participants.extracted: Men aged 65 years and older with symptoms and signs consistent with androgen deficiency and low serum testosterone concentrations (<275 ng/dL on two morning measures) were enrolled in the Testosterone Trials Cardiovascular Trial. Participants also had subjective complaints and objective evidence of sexual dysfunction, physical dysfunction, and/or reduced vitality, and were not at elevated risk for prostate, cardiovascular, or hematologic complications from testosterone therapy. For the cardiovascular substudy, men additionally required normal baseline renal function (eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2) and exclusion of conditions such as weight >300 lb, allergy to iodinated contrast, inability to breath-hold, tachyarrhythmia (e.g., atrial fibrillation), or prior coronary artery bypass grafting. In total, 199 men consented to screening, 170 were eligible, and 165 had a baseline coronary CT angiography scan; the mean age of participants was 71.1 years, most were overweight or obese, and common comorbidities included hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and diabetes.

interventions.extracted: Participants in the Cardiovascular Trial of the Testosterone Trials received daily transdermal AndroGel® (testosterone gel in an alcohol-water base) or a matched placebo gel for one year. Treatment allocation used a minimization algorithm with a random component, and doses were adjusted by the unblinded data coordinating center to maintain testosterone concentrations between 500 and 800 ng/mL; matching adjustments were made in placebo participants to preserve blinding.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure coronary atherosclerosis outcomes by serial coronary computed tomographic angiography (CCTA) over 12 months. The primary outcome was percent change in non-calcified coronary plaque volume from baseline to 12 months. Secondary outcomes included change in total plaque volume, change in coronary artery calcium (CAC) score, presence of any CAC (dichotomous), change in left ventricular mass, and changes in metabolic measures including hemoglobin A1c and homeostatic model assessment of insulin resistance (HOMA-IR) in participants without diabetes at baseline. Imaging-based outcomes were assessed at baseline and at 12 months, with comparisons made between testosterone and placebo groups.
